title: Universal Approximation Constraints of Narrow ResNets: The Tunnel Effect
abstract: We analyze the universal approximation constraints of narrow Residual Neural Networks (ResNets) both theoretically and numerically. For deep neural networks without input space augmentation, a central constraint is the inability to represent critical points of the input-output map. We prove that this has global consequences for target function approximations and show that the manifestation of this defect is typically a shift of the critical point to infinity, which we call the ``tunnel effect'' in the context of classification tasks. While ResNets offer greater expressivity than standard multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), their capability strongly depends on the signal ratio between the skip and residual channels. We establish quantitative approximation bounds for both the residual-dominant (close to MLP) and skip-dominant (close to neural ODE) regimes. These estimates depend explicitly on the channel ratio and uniform network weight bounds. Low-dimensional examples further provide a detailed analysis of the different ResNet regimes and how architecture-target incompatibility influences the approximation error.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Universal approximation theory of neural networks has initially focused on shallow networks of arbitrary width [ Cybenko1989 , Hornik1989 ] . However, the success of deep neural networks has shifted interest toward architectures characterized by bounded width and significant depth [ LuPuWang2017expressive ] . As a result, research in recent years focused on determining the expressive power of these practical implementations where layer capacity is limited.

It is established that both feedforward neural networks and neural ODEs are unable to approximate certain functions well for narrow architectures where the natural input space is not augmented [ Kuehn2023 , Dupont2019 ] . Mathematically, a central issue is the inability of these models’ input-output maps to express critical (or stationary) points. This topological constraint does not just have local consequences but limits global approximation and generalization capabilities. The classification of the two-dimensional circle toy dataset illustrates this limitation: a trained MLP with two neurons per layer and an arbitrary number of layers generates a “tunnel” in the prediction level sets which leads to unavoidable misclassifications, see Figure 1 . Since the model structure cannot express a critical point, it must shift the critical point to infinity which creates the barrier. Remarkably, the same observation holds for the structurally very different neural ODEs, where the input-output map is realized through continuous trajectories [ Dupont2019 , Chen2018 ] .

The structure of Residual Neural Networks (ResNets) [ he16 ] can be understood as “in-between” classical feedforward neural networks (such as MLPs) and neural ODEs, and is able to resolve these topological constraints [ lj18resnet ] . The idea of ResNets is to split each layer into an identity channel that simply advances the layer input unchanged and a residual channel that is a nonlinear transformation containing the trainable parameters. The layer update is formulated as h l = h l − 1 + f ​ ( h l − 1 , θ l ) h_{l}=h_{l-1}+f(h_{l-1},\theta_{l}) , such that the network learns the difference between the layer input and the layer output rather than the full transformation. This approach enables the training of very deep architectures as it provides stable gradient computations [ haber2017 , Weinan2017 ] . Beyond these remarkable optimization benefits, it is striking that the architecture also improves the expressivity of the input-output map and is able to embed critical points without additional input augmentation. However, we observe that this ability depends rather strongly on the choice of parameter initialization. Let us again consider the circle toy dataset. Training a ResNet of width two, moderate depth, and standard weight initialization for sigmoidal activations, the “tunnel effect” of Figure 1 typically does not appear. By contrast, when the parameters are rescaled closer to 0 at initialization, the “tunnel” appears after training.

This work proves that the “tunnel effect” appears whenever ResNets are unable to embed critical points but try to approximate target functions that contain such points. It further provides explicit estimates on the embedding restrictions of ResNets in the model parameter regimes close to MLPs and neural ODEs. This implies that ResNets that roughly maintain a balance between the identity channel and the transformation channel have advantageous expressivity in practical settings. We formulate these results by parameterizing the ratio between the identity and residual channel of each ResNet layer.

sections.heading: 2Critical Points in Residual Neural Networks
sections.text:
We begin in Section 2.1 to recall the universal approximation and universal embedding properties of continuous input–output mappings relevant to our setting. We show that, for such mappings, the inability to represent a critical point in a single output coordinate implies that the universal approximation property cannot hold.

Section 2.2 introduces the Residual Neural Network (ResNet) architectures considered in this work, whose layers depend on a skip parameter ε \varepsilon and a residual parameter δ \delta . Depending on the input and hidden dimensions, we distinguish between non-augmented and augmented architectures.

Section 2.3 states the specific assumptions on ResNets that are necessary for our analysis in the remaining part of this work.

In Section 2.4 , we derive the ResNet input gradient. In the non-augmented case, we identify conditions under which the gradient never vanishes. In particular, we prove that if the ratio α ≔ δ ε \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon} is sufficiently small or sufficiently large, the network admits no critical points.

Finally, Section 2.5 examines the topological consequences of the absence of critical points. For small α \alpha , the networks can be interpreted as discretizations of neural ODEs, whereas for large α \alpha , they act as perturbations of classical feed-forward neural networks (FNNs). In both regimes, we derive bounds on the distance to the respective limiting models and discuss the resulting topological restrictions.

sections.heading: 2.1Universal Approximation and Critical Points
sections.text:
Our goal in this work is to study the expressivity of ResNets depending on the specific architectural choices. For that purpose, we specialize the general definitions of universal approximation and universal embedding from [ kk2025 ] to the Euclidean setting, focusing on the space C k ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) C^{k}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open, of k k -times continuously differentiable functions f : 𝒳 → ℝ n out f:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}} . We use the max-norm ‖ y ‖ ∞ ≔ max i ∈ { 1 , … , n } ⁡ | y i | \left\lVert y\right\rVert_{\infty}\coloneqq\max_{i\in\{1,\ldots,n\}}\left\lvert y_{i}\right\rvert for vectors y ∈ ℝ n y\in\mathbb{R}^{n} and denote the induced sup-norm of f ∈ C k ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) f\in C^{k}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) on a set 𝒟 ⊂ 𝒳 \mathcal{D}\subset\mathcal{X} by ‖ f ‖ ∞ , 𝒟 ≔ sup x ∈ 𝒟 ‖ f ​ ( x ) ‖ ∞ = sup x ∈ 𝒟 max i ∈ { 1 , … , n out } ⁡ | f i ​ ( x ) | . \left\lVert f\right\rVert_{\infty,\mathcal{D}}\coloneqq\sup_{x\in\mathcal{D}}\left\lVert f(x)\right\rVert_{\infty}=\sup_{x\in\mathcal{D}}\max_{i\in\{1,\ldots,n_{\textup{out}}\}}\left\lvert f_{i}(x)\right\rvert.

As we study in the upcoming sections the input-output map of different neural network architectures directly, it is useful to additionally introduce the stronger concept of universal embedding, which requires an exact representation of the target function.

An important property characterizing the dynamics of the input-output map of neural networks Φ ∈ C 1 ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) \Phi\in C^{1}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) with input space 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open and output space ℝ n out \mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}} , is the existence of critical points, i.e., zeros of the network input gradient ∇ x Φ \nabla_{x}\Phi . It is crucial not to confuse the input gradient ∇ x Φ \nabla_{x}\Phi with the parameter gradient ∇ θ Φ \nabla_{\theta}\Phi , where θ ∈ Θ \theta\in\Theta denotes all parameters of the considered neural network. The parameter gradient ∇ θ Φ \nabla_{\theta}\Phi is needed for backpropagation algorithms in the training process of neural networks.

It is instructive to study the existence of critical points in the input-output map of different neural network architectures, as this has direct implications for their embedding and approximation capabilities [ kk2025 ] . The following theorem implies that neural network architectures in which at least one component map has no critical point cannot have the universal approximation property.

As Theorem 2.3 considers maps in which single output components cannot have any critical points, we can, without loss of generality, restrict our upcoming analysis to scalar neural network architectures with n out = 1 n_{\textup{out}}=1 .
In the recent work [ kk2025 ] , the expressivity of multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and neural ODEs is studied by characterizing the existence and regularity of critical points. One of the main results shows that non-augmented MLPs and non-augmented neural ODEs cannot have critical points and hence lack the universal approximation property (see also Section 3.3 and Section 4.3 ). In the augmented case, the universal approximation property of MLPs and neural ODEs is well established in the literature [ Dupont2019 , Hornik1989 , Hornik1991 , Kidger2022 , Pinkus1999 , Zhang2020a ] .
In the following sections, we first introduce ResNet architectures and then study their expressivity using Theorem 2.3 .

sections.heading: 2.2Residual Neural Networks
sections.text:
Residual neural networks (ResNets) are feedforward neural networks structured in layers h l ∈ ℝ n hid h_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} , l ∈ { 1 , … , L } l\in\{1,\ldots,L\} , consisting of n hid n_{\textup{hid}} nodes each. Given an initial layer h 0 ∈ ℝ n hid h_{0}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} , the hidden layers are iteratively updated by h l = ε ​ h l − 1 + δ ​ f l ​ ( h l − 1 , θ l ) , l ∈ { 1 , … , L } , h_{l}=\varepsilon h_{l-1}+\delta f_{l}(h_{l-1},\theta_{l}),\qquad l\in\{1,\ldots,L\}, (2.2) with a (typically nonlinear) residual function f l : ℝ n hid × ℝ p l → ℝ n hid f_{l}:\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}}\times\mathbb{R}^{p_{l}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} and hidden parameters θ l ∈ Θ l ⊂ ℝ p l \theta_{l}\in\Theta_{l}\subset\mathbb{R}^{p_{l}} , where Θ l \Theta_{l} denotes the set of parameters of layer l l and Θ = Θ 1 × … × Θ L ⊂ ℝ p \Theta=\Theta_{1}\times\ldots\times\Theta_{L}\subset\mathbb{R}^{p} the total parameter space of the ResNet. The layer update rule ( 2.2 ) includes two terms: a skip connection scaled by the skip parameter ε > 0 \varepsilon>0 , and a residual term weighted by the residual parameter δ > 0 \delta>0 .

In contrast to classical feed-forward neural networks, corresponding to the case ε = 0 \varepsilon=0 and δ > 0 \delta>0 in ( 2.2 ) (cf. Section 4 ), the ResNet update rule contains the linear term ε ​ h l − 1 \varepsilon h_{l-1} . It is called a skip- or shortcut connection (cf. [ he16 ] ), as it allows the layer input to bypass the transformation of the residual function, see Figure 3 3(a) for a visualization. The residual function f l f_{l} can be an arbitrary map, but typical choices include f l ​ ( h l − 1 , θ l ) ≔ W ~ l ​ σ l ​ ( W l ​ h l − 1 + b l ) + b ~ l = W ~ l ​ σ l ​ ( a l ) + b ~ l f_{l}(h_{l-1},\theta_{l})\coloneqq\widetilde{W}_{l}\sigma_{l}(W_{l}h_{l-1}+b_{l})+\tilde{b}_{l}=\widetilde{W}_{l}\sigma_{l}(a_{l})+\tilde{b}_{l} (2.3) for l ∈ { 1 , … , L } l\in\{1,\ldots,L\} with parameters θ l ≔ ( W l , W ~ l , b l , b ~ l ) \theta_{l}\coloneqq(W_{l},\widetilde{W}_{l},b_{l},\tilde{b}_{l}) consisting of weight matrices W l ∈ ℝ m l × n hid W_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}\times n_{\textup{hid}}} , W ~ l ∈ ℝ n hid × m l \widetilde{W}_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}\times m_{l}} , and biases b l ∈ ℝ m l b_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}} , b ~ l ∈ ℝ n hid \tilde{b}_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} . The map σ l : ℝ → ℝ \sigma_{l}:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} is called an activation function , applied component-wise to the pre-activated states a l ≔ W l ​ h l − 1 + b l ∈ ℝ m l a_{l}\coloneqq W_{l}h_{l-1}+b_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}} , as visualized in Figure 3 3(b) . By a slight abuse of notation, we also write σ l \sigma_{l} for the component-wise extension σ l : ℝ m l → ℝ m l \sigma_{l}:\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}} as in ( 2.3 ). The intended meaning will be clear from the argument. Typical choices for the activation function σ l \sigma_{l} include tanh \tanh , sigmoid ⁡ ( y ) = ( 1 + e − y ) − 1 \operatorname{sigmoid}(y)=(1+e^{-y})^{-1} or ReLU ⁡ ( y ) = max ⁡ { 0 , y } \operatorname{ReLU}(y)=\max\{0,y\} . The hidden dimension n hid n_{\textup{hid}} is constant across the layers of the ResNet, whereas the dimension m l m_{l} of the pre-activated state a l a_{l} , which also controls the number of parameters used in layer h l h_{l} , can vary.

To be flexible in the input and output dimensions of ResNets, two additional transformations are applied before the layer h 0 h_{0} and after the layer h L h_{L} , resulting in the input-output map Φ : 𝒳 → ℝ n out , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in , Φ ​ ( x ) = λ ~ ​ ( h L ​ ( λ ​ ( x ) ) ) , \Phi:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}},\;\mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}},\qquad\Phi(x)=\tilde{\lambda}(h_{L}(\lambda(x))), (2.4) with input transformation λ : ℝ n in → ℝ n hid \lambda:\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} , output transformation λ ~ : ℝ n hid → ℝ n out \tilde{\lambda}:\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}} and composite map h L : ℝ n hid → ℝ n hid h_{L}:\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} , which maps the transformed input h 0 = λ ​ ( x ) h_{0}=\lambda(x) to the last hidden layer h L h_{L} via the iterative update rule ( 2.2 ). For the ResNet architecture ( 2.4 ), we call x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} the input , h 0 = λ ​ ( x ) h_{0}=\lambda(x) the transformed input , h 1 , … , h L h_{1},\ldots,h_{L} the hidden layers , and Φ ​ ( x ) \Phi(x) the output of the neural network. Often, the transformations λ \lambda and λ ~ \tilde{\lambda} are chosen to be affine linear, but nonlinear functions are also possible. For example, for classification tasks with n out = 1 n_{\textup{out}}=1 , the output is often normalized to a probability, i.e., Φ ​ ( x ) ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] \Phi(x)\in[0,1] .

The case ε = δ = 1 \varepsilon=\delta=1 in the update rule ( 2.2 ) corresponds to classical ResNet architectures as introduced in [ he16 ] . The general case with ε , δ > 0 \varepsilon,\delta>0 can also be seen as a special case of highway networks with carry gate ε \varepsilon and transform gate δ \delta [ Srivastava2015 ] . In the case ε = 1 \varepsilon=1 and δ → 0 \delta\rightarrow 0 , ResNets are connected to neural ODEs, which we introduce in Section 3 . The case δ > 0 \delta>0 and ε = 0 \varepsilon=0 with residual function f l f_{l} as defined in ( 2.3 ) leads to classical feed-forward neural networks, such as multilayer perceptrons, introduced in Section 4 . In our upcoming analysis, we focus on ResNets with ε , δ > 0 \varepsilon,\delta>0 , which include both a linear skip connection and a non-linear residual term. In the following, we define the class of considered ResNet architectures.

The regularity of ResNets Φ ∈ RN ε , δ k ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) \Phi\in\textup{RN}_{\varepsilon,\delta}^{k}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) , k ≥ 0 k\geq 0 , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open, follows directly from the regularity of the residual functions f l f_{l} or the activation functions σ l \sigma_{l} , respectively.

Depending on the input dimension n in n_{\textup{in}} and the hidden dimension n hid n_{\textup{hid}} , we distinguish between non-augmented and augmented ResNet architectures.

The concept of non-augmented and augmented ResNets is visualized in Figure 4 . The classification of ResNets is independent of the choice of the residual functions f l f_{l} , and hence also independent of the intermediate dimensions m l m_{l} in the case of canonical ResNets. The distinction of architectures becomes relevant for the analysis of the expressivity of the ResNet input-output map in the upcoming Section 2.4 and Section 2.5 . Our work mainly focuses on the restrictions of non-augmented ResNet architectures induced by Theorem 2.3 . Before we calculate the ResNet input gradient in Section 2.4 to characterize the existence of critical points, we discuss in the following Section 2.3 the assumptions on non-augmented architectures relevant for our analysis.

sections.heading: 2.3Assumptions on Non-Augmented Architectures
sections.text: In this section, we state the assumptions used for the analysis of non-augmented ResNet architectures in Section 2.4 and Section 2.5 . These conditions also appear in the study of ResNets related to neural ODEs (Section 3 ) and to feed-forward neural networks (Section 4 ). The assumptions are introduced below for reference; each of the upcoming theorems explicitly states which of the assumptions are required.

sections.heading: 2.4Existence of Critical Points
sections.text:
To understand the existence of critical points in ResNet architectures, we derive in the following the input gradient of ResNets Φ ∈ RN ε , δ 1 ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ ) \Phi\in\textup{RN}_{\varepsilon,\delta}^{1}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}) , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open. Due to Theorem 2.3 , we can without loss of generality restrict our analysis to single output components, or equivalently, scalar neural networks. The upcoming proposition applies to both non-augmented and augmented ResNets.

To apply Theorem 2.3 to residual architectures, we formulate the criterion for non-vanishing gradients via the rank of the layer-wise Jacobians. The following lemma shows that the rank solely depends on the ratio α ≔ δ ε \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon} of the residual parameter δ \delta and the skip parameter ε \varepsilon and not on their individual size.

In the case of non-augmented ResNets Φ ∈ RN ε , δ , N 1 ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ ) \Phi\in\textup{RN}^{1}_{\varepsilon,\delta,\textup{N}}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}) , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open, Lemma 2.12 allows us to formulate conditions on the transformations λ \lambda , λ ~ \tilde{\lambda} and the parameters ε \varepsilon and δ \delta , under which the gradient ∇ x Φ ​ ( x ) \nabla_{x}\Phi(x) never vanishes for any x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} .

Depending on the ratio α ≔ δ ε \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon} between the skip parameter ε \varepsilon and the residual parameter δ \delta , we analyze in Section 3.3 and Section 4.3 when the first assumption of Proposition 2.13 is fulfilled. We identify two parameter regimes, 0 < α ≪ 1 0<\alpha\ll 1 and α ≫ 1 \alpha\gg 1 , for which non-augmented ResNets cannot have any critical points, as visualized in Figure 2 . In these parameter regimes, ResNets lack the universal approximation property as implied by Theorem 2.3 . Consequently, we extend the results of [ kk2025 ] , regarding the non-existence of critical points in non-augmented neural ODEs and non-augmented MLPs, to ResNets.

In intermediate parameter regimes of α \alpha , non-augmented ResNets can have the universal approximation property, as demonstrated in [ Lin2018 ] for the standard case ε = δ = 1 \varepsilon=\delta=1 . In contrast, for augmented ResNet architectures, no equivalent statement to Proposition 2.13 about the non-existence of critical points exists. Instead, it follows analogously to [ kk2025 , Theorem 3.18] that due to dimension augmentation, critical points can exist in augmented architectures even when all other assumptions of Proposition 2.13 are fulfilled.

The following table provides an overview of the main results of this work for non-augmented ResNets in the parameter regimes 0 < α ≪ 1 0<\alpha\ll 1 and α ≫ 1 \alpha\gg 1 .

While the previous results establish the absence of critical points in specific regimes, we now turn to a global topological perspective. In the following, we quantify the expressivity of non-augmented ResNets by measuring their distance to function classes that are already known to lack universal approximation.

sections.heading: 2.5Global Topological Restrictions
sections.text:
In Section 2.4 , we discussed that non-augmented ResNets cannot have critical points in the parameter regimes 0 < α ≪ 1 0<\alpha\ll 1 and α ≫ 1 \alpha\gg 1 . Complementing that direct analysis, we characterize the expressivity of ResNets by comparing them to reference networks Φ ¯ \overline{\Phi} without critical points, such as non-augmented neural ODEs (cf. Theorem 3.9 ) and non-augmented FNNs (cf. Theorem 4.16 ).
In Section 3.2 , we show that the distance between ResNets with ε = 1 \varepsilon=1 and neural ODEs scales linearly in the residual parameter δ \delta . Similarly, in Section 4.2 , we establish that for fixed δ > 0 \delta>0 and small ε > 0 \varepsilon>0 , the distance between ResNets and FNNs scales asymptotically linearly in the skip parameter ε \varepsilon .

We divide the upcoming analysis into two steps. First, we establish the topological restrictions of a continuously differentiable map Φ ¯ \overline{\Phi} lacking critical points. Second, we introduce a uniform distance bound to show that these topological restrictions extend to any continuous neural network Φ \Phi that closely approximates Φ ¯ \overline{\Phi} .

sections.heading: 3Case0<α≪10<\alpha\ll 1: Close to Neural ODEs
sections.text: In Section 3.1 , we introduce neural ordinary differential equations (neural ODEs). Next, we motivate the study of neural ODEs in Section 3.2 by showing that neural ODEs and ResNets with sufficiently small α ≔ δ ε \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon} can be interpreted as discrete and continuous counterparts of the same underlying dynamics. Additionally, we calculate the approximation error between the neural ODE and ResNet dynamics arising from an explicit Euler discretization. Finally, we show in Section 3.3 that, in analogy to non-augmented neural ODEs, critical points cannot exist in non-augmented ResNets if 0 < α ≪ 1 0<\alpha\ll 1 .

sections.heading: 3.1Neural ODEs
sections.text:
In this section, we introduce neural ODEs, which can be interpreted as the infinite-depth limit of ResNets. In analogy to ResNets, we include two additional transformations before and after the initial value problem, and we distinguish between non-augmented and augmented architectures.
We consider neural ODEs based on the solution h : [ 0 , T ] → ℝ n hid h:[0,T]\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} of an initial value problem d ​ h d ​ t = f ​ ( h ​ ( t ) , θ ​ ( t ) ) , h ​ ( 0 ) = a ∈ 𝒜 ⊂ ℝ n hid , \frac{\mathrm{d}h}{\mathrm{d}t}=f(h(t),\theta(t)),\qquad h(0)=a\in\mathcal{A}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}}, (3.1) with a non-autonomous vector field f : ℝ n hid × ℝ p → ℝ n hid f:\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}}\times\mathbb{R}^{p}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} , parameter function θ ∈ Θ NODE ⊂ C 0 ​ ( [ 0 , T ] , ℝ p ) \theta\in\Theta_{\text{NODE}}\subset C^{0}([0,T],\mathbb{R}^{p}) , and a non-empty set of possible initial conditions 𝒜 ⊂ ℝ n hid \mathcal{A}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} . When required, the solution with initial condition h ​ ( 0 ) = a h(0)=a is denoted by h a ​ ( t ) h_{a}(t) to emphasize the dependence on the initial condition.

As with ResNets, we allow flexibility regarding the input and output dimensions of neural ODEs. We define neural ODEs as the input-output maps given by Φ ¯ : 𝒳 → ℝ n out , Φ ¯ ​ ( x ) = λ ~ ​ ( h λ ​ ( x ) ​ ( T ) ) , \overline{\Phi}:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}},\qquad\overline{\Phi}(x)=\tilde{\lambda}(h_{\lambda(x)}(T)), (3.2) with 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open, input transformation λ : ℝ n in → ℝ n hid \lambda:\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}} , and output transformation λ ~ : ℝ n hid → ℝ n out \tilde{\lambda}:\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{hid}}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}} . To more easily distinguish between neural ODE and ResNet architectures, we denote them in this section as Φ ¯ \overline{\Phi} and Φ \Phi , respectively. As with ResNets, λ \lambda and λ ~ \tilde{\lambda} are often chosen to be affine linear, but they can also be nonlinear maps. The architecture ( 3.2 ) depends on the time- T T map h λ ​ ( x ) ​ ( T ) h_{\lambda(x)}(T) of the initial value problem ( 3.1 ). We assume that for every a ∈ 𝒜 a\in\mathcal{A} , the solution of ( 3.1 ) exists on the entire time interval [ 0 , T ] [0,T] . In the upcoming definition, we denote by C i , j ​ ( 𝒳 × 𝒴 , 𝒵 ) C^{i,j}(\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y},\mathcal{Z}) the space of functions f : 𝒳 × 𝒴 → 𝒵 f:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y}\rightarrow\mathcal{Z} that are i i -times continuously differentiable in the first variable and j j -times continuously differentiable in the second variable.

The set of possible initial conditions of ( 3.1 ) is defined by 𝒜 ≔ λ ​ ( 𝒳 ) \mathcal{A}\coloneqq\lambda(\mathcal{X}) , as the input x ∈ 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in x\in\mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} of the neural ODE is mapped under the transformation λ \lambda to a = λ ​ ( x ) a=\lambda(x) . The regularity of neural ODEs Φ ¯ ∈ NODE k ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) \overline{\Phi}\in\textup{NODE}^{k}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) follows directly from the regularity of the vector field f f , as explained in [ kk2025 , Lemma 4.3] . We restrict Definition 3.1 to the case k ≥ 1 k\geq 1 to guarantee uniqueness of solution curves of ( 3.1 ) and hence well-posedness of the neural ODEs.

The following classification of non-augmented and augmented neural ODEs is independent of the choice of the vector field f f and applies to all neural ODEs Φ ¯ ∈ NODE k ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) \overline{\Phi}\in\textup{NODE}^{k}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) . The concept of non-augmented and augmented neural ODEs is illustrated in Figure 8 .

sections.heading: 3.2Relationship to ResNets
sections.text:
The following proposition demonstrates that, given our precise definitions, ResNets (cf. Definition 2.4 ) and neural ODEs (cf. Definition 3.1 ) can be understood as discrete and continuous counterparts of the same underlying dynamics. This is based on the well-established observation that ResNets can be interpreted as the explicit Euler discretization of neural ODEs [ Weinan2017 ] .

The proof of this proposition relies on standard arguments and mainly consists of translating the notation between ResNets and discretized neural ODEs. It is therefore proven in Appendix A.1 and included for completeness. The following remark argues that the assumptions of Proposition 3.4 (b) are not restrictive in typical settings.

In the following, we explicitly calculate the approximation error between the ResNet and neural ODE architectures considered, thereby adapting [ Sander2022 , Proposition 1] to the more general neural ODE architecture of Definition 3.1 . The following theorem shows that the global error of the input-output map between neural ODEs and their explicit Euler discretization depends linearly on the step size or residual parameter δ \delta . Unless stated otherwise, we always consider Lipschitz constants with respect to the max-norm.

We now specialize Theorem 3.6 to canonical ResNets with the typical residual function ( 2.3 ). The proof consists of explicitly computing the constants M θ M_{\theta} and K θ K_{\theta} and is given in Appendix B.1 .

The preceding theorem and corollary show that, in the limit δ → 0 \delta\rightarrow 0 , neural ODEs and the corresponding ResNets can be viewed as continuous and discrete realizations of the same underlying dynamics. In the setting of Theorem 3.6 , this correspondence holds for ResNets with skip parameter ε = 1 \varepsilon=1 . More generally, ResNets with arbitrary skip parameter ε ≠ 1 \varepsilon\neq 1 are also related to neural ODEs, as discussed in Proposition 3.4 (b) . In this general case, taking the limit δ → 0 \delta\rightarrow 0 for fixed ε > 0 \varepsilon>0 means α ≔ δ ε → 0 \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon}\rightarrow 0 , as also discussed in Sections 2.4 and 2.5 .

sections.heading: 3.3Existence of Critical Points
sections.text:
In this section, we characterize the existence of critical points in neural ODEs and ResNets in the parameter regime 0 < α ≪ 1 0<\alpha\ll 1 . For that purpose, we first extend the result of [ kk2025 ] that non-augmented neural ODEs cannot have any critical points to our setting of general input and output transformations.

In the following theorem, we show for non-augmented ResNets Φ ∈ RN ε , δ , N 1 ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ ) \Phi\in\textup{RN}^{1}_{\varepsilon,\delta,\textup{N}}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}) , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open, that the property that no critical points exist, persists for α ≔ δ ε \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon} sufficiently small. In the case of canonical ResNets we provide an explicit upper bound on the ratio α \alpha , below which Φ \Phi cannot have any critical points.

sections.heading: 4Caseα≫1\alpha\gg 1: Close to Feed-Forward Neural Networks
sections.text: In Section 4.1 , we define general feed-forward neural networks (FNNs) and their canonical form, given by multilayer perceptrons (MLPs). In Section 4.2 , we link ResNets to FNNs by taking the parameter limit α ≔ δ ε → ∞ \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon}\rightarrow\infty , corresponding to a ResNet with dominating residual term. Additionally, we calculate the approximation error between FNNs and ResNets for large residual terms (large α \alpha ). Finally, in Section 4.3 we show that (in analogy to non-augmented MLPs) critical points cannot exist in non-augmented ResNets for α \alpha sufficiently large.

sections.heading: 4.1Feed-Forward Neural Networks
sections.text:
Classical feed-forward neural networks, such as MLPs, are structured in consecutive layers. In the following, we introduce the general FNN architecture we study, which, unlike ResNets, has no skip connection. We introduce FNNs with L + 2 L+2 layers to align with the notation introduced for ResNets.
General FNNs are structured in layers h l ∈ ℝ n l h_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{l}} defined by the iterative update rule h l = δ ​ f l ​ ( h l − 1 , θ l ) , l ∈ { 0 , … , L + 1 } , h_{l}=\delta f_{l}(h_{l-1},\theta_{l}),\qquad l\in\{0,\ldots,L+1\}, (4.1) with input h − 1 ≔ x ∈ 𝒳 h_{-1}\coloneqq x\in\mathcal{X} , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} , a (typically nonlinear) layer map f l : ℝ n l − 1 × ℝ p l → ℝ n l f_{l}:\mathbb{R}^{n_{l-1}}\times\mathbb{R}^{p_{l}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{l}} , and parameters θ l ∈ Θ l ⊂ ℝ p l \theta_{l}\in\Theta_{l}\subset\mathbb{R}^{p_{l}} , where Θ l \Theta_{l} denotes the set of parameters of layer l l . As in the definition of ResNets, we include the scaling parameter δ > 0 \delta>0 . In the case of MLPs, the layer map has the explicit form f l ​ ( h l − 1 , θ l ) ≔ W ~ l ​ σ l ​ ( W l ​ h l − 1 + b l ) + b ~ l = W ~ l ​ σ l ​ ( a l ) + b ~ l f_{l}(h_{l-1},\theta_{l})\coloneqq\widetilde{W}_{l}\sigma_{l}(W_{l}h_{l-1}+b_{l})+\tilde{b}_{l}=\widetilde{W}_{l}\sigma_{l}(a_{l})+\tilde{b}_{l} (4.2) where W ~ l ∈ ℝ n l × m l \widetilde{W}_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{l}\times m_{l}} , W l ∈ ℝ m l × n l − 1 W_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}\times n_{l-1}} are weight matrices, b l ∈ ℝ m l b_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}} , b ~ l ∈ ℝ n l \tilde{b}_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{l}} are biases, h − 1 ≔ x h_{-1}\coloneqq x is the input data and σ l : ℝ → ℝ \sigma_{l}:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} is a component-wise applied activation function. As for ResNets, we write σ l \sigma_{l} for the component-wise extension σ l : ℝ m l → ℝ m l \sigma_{l}:\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}} , where m l m_{l} is the dimension of the pre-activated states a l ≔ W l ​ h l − 1 + b l a_{l}\coloneqq W_{l}h_{l-1}+b_{l} , l ∈ { 0 , … , L + 1 } l\in\{0,\ldots,L+1\} . The layer map in ( 4.2 ) agrees with the residual functions of canonical ResNets defined in ( 2.3 ) up to the layer-dependent dimension n l n_{l} . We denote the FNN input-output map by Φ ¯ : 𝒳 → ℝ n out , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in , Φ ¯ ​ ( x ) = h L + 1 ​ ( x ) \overline{\Phi}:\mathcal{X}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}},\;\mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}},\qquad\overline{\Phi}(x)=h_{L+1}(x) (4.3) with n out ≔ n L + 1 n_{\textup{out}}\coloneqq n_{L+1} . To more easily distinguish general feed-forward architectures (including MLPs) from ResNet architectures, we denote them in this section by Φ ¯ \overline{\Phi} and Φ \Phi , respectively. In contrast to ResNets, where the update rule ( 2.2 ) requires constant layer width, the layer widths of FNNs can change. As the input and output dimensions n in ≔ n − 1 n_{\textup{in}}\coloneqq n_{-1} and n out ≔ n L + 1 n_{\textup{out}}\coloneqq n_{L+1} are for FNNs not required to be equal, we do not include two additional transformations λ \lambda , λ ~ \tilde{\lambda} to the architecture as for ResNets in ( 2.4 ).

In Section 4.2 , we discuss how FNNs are related to ResNets with ε = 0 \varepsilon=0 , and how MLPs connect to canonical ResNets with ε = 0 \varepsilon=0 .

Depending on the dimensions n l n_{l} of the layers h l ∈ ℝ n l h_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{n_{l}} and the dimensions m l m_{l} of the pre-activated states a l ∈ ℝ m l a_{l}\in\mathbb{R}^{m_{l}} , we define non-augmented FNNs and MLPs as follows.

The concept of non-augmented FNNs and MLPs is visualized in Figure 9 .

While FNNs allow for arbitrary layer maps, we now focus on the algebraic structure of MLPs.

Given an MLP, the following theorem allows us to always suppose that Assumption (B2) holds, i.e., that all weight matrices have full rank.

The assumption that W 0 W_{0} and W ~ L + 1 \widetilde{W}_{L+1} have full rank is analogous to Assumption (C1) that the Jacobian matrices of the input and output transformation have full rank. The proof of [ kk2025 , Theorem 3.10] replaces the matrix product W l + 1 ​ W ~ l W_{l+1}\widetilde{W}_{l} in ( 4.4 ) by a product of full rank matrices of possibly smaller dimensions. For ResNet architectures, it is not possible to define a normal form in the same way.

sections.heading: 4.2Relationship to ResNets
sections.text:
In the following proposition, we show that given a ResNet Φ ∈ RN 0 , δ k ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) \Phi\in\textup{RN}_{0,\delta}^{k}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} with skip parameter ε = 0 \varepsilon=0 , there exists an FNN Φ ¯ ∈ FNN δ k ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ n out ) \overline{\Phi}\in\textup{FNN}_{\delta}^{k}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{out}}}) , that has the same input-output map as Φ \Phi . Conversely, the statement also holds if the FNN’s hidden layers have a constant width. In the previous section, we introduced FNNs with L + 2 L+2 layers to maintain the analogy with ResNets, which have, in addition to the L L hidden layers, one input and one output transformation.

The proof of this proposition only relies on the translation of the notation between ResNets and FNNs. It is therefore proven in Appendix A.2 and included for completeness. Proposition 4.9 shows that the ResNet and FNN dynamics coincide in the case ε = 0 \varepsilon=0 , which relates to the limit α ≔ δ ε → ∞ \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon}\rightarrow\infty , as ε → 0 \varepsilon\rightarrow 0 for fixed δ > 0 \delta>0 , as also discussed in Section 2.4 and Section 2.5 .

In the following, we aim to quantify the distance between FNNs and ResNets with a small skip parameter 0 < ε < 1 0<\varepsilon<1 . For this purpose, we first calculate the explicit input-output map of ResNets. Although Definition 2.4 assumes ε , δ > 0 \varepsilon,\delta>0 , the following lemma includes the limiting cases ε = 0 \varepsilon=0 and δ = 0 \delta=0 .

The following theorem shows that the global error of the input-output map between ResNets and FNNs scales linearly in the skip parameter ε \varepsilon asymptotically for ε → 0 \varepsilon\rightarrow 0 and fixed δ > 0 \delta>0 and L > 0 L>0 .

We now consider Theorem 4.12 for canonical ResNets with the typical residual function ( 2.3 ). The proof consists of explicitly computing the constants S f S_{f} , K f K_{f} , S λ S_{\lambda} and K λ ~ K_{\tilde{\lambda}} and is given in Appendix B.2 .

sections.heading: 4.3Existence of Critical Points
sections.text:
In this section, we characterize the existence of critical points in FNNs and ResNets in the parameter regime α ≫ 1 \alpha\gg 1 . For that purpose, we first extend a result of [ kk2025 ] , that non-augmented MLPs in normal form cannot have any critical points, to our setting of general layer maps.

In the following theorem, we show for non-augmented ResNets Φ ∈ RN ε , δ , N 1 ​ ( 𝒳 , ℝ ) \Phi\in\textup{RN}^{1}_{\varepsilon,\delta,\textup{N}}(\mathcal{X},\mathbb{R}) , 𝒳 ⊂ ℝ n in \mathcal{X}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n_{\textup{in}}} open, that the absence of critical points persists for α ≔ δ ε \alpha\coloneqq\frac{\delta}{\varepsilon} sufficiently large. In the case of canonical ResNets, we provide an explicit lower bound on the ratio α \alpha above which Φ \Phi cannot have any critical points.

sections.heading: 5Examples
sections.text: We start with a thorough analysis of the simplest one-dimensional ResNets, as they illustrate the embedding restrictions present for the various regimes of α \alpha in their fundamental form. This has two purposes. First, to present the application of the derived expressivity results of Section 2 to Section 4 to explicit examples. Second, to discuss the impact of the parameter initialization and training on the embedding restrictions. We further implement two-dimensional classification tasks that illustrate the connection of the approximation constraints to the “tunnel effect” proven in Theorem 2.18 . The code used to generate the models and resulting plots of this section can be found at https://github.com/twoehrer/Narrow_ResNet_Constraints.git .

sections.heading: 5.1One-Dimensional ResNets
sections.text:
For this entire subsection, our goal is to use one-dimensional ResNets to approximate the function f ​ ( x ) = x 2 f(x)=x^{2} for x ∈ ( − 1 , 1 ) x\in(-1,1) as it has a critical point at x = 0 x=0 .

Before analyzing this one-dimensional setting in detail, let us state the implementations’ numerical specifications : The models are trained on 300 uniformly distributed points, x i ∼ 𝒰 ​ ( − 1 , 1 ) x_{i}\sim\mathcal{U}(-1,1) , with labels y i = x i 2 y_{i}=x_{i}^{2} for i = 1 , … , 300 i=1,\ldots,300 . A mean-squared error loss is used to train the networks to approximate the target function. We use standard Xavier initialization (a standard normal distribution in the one-dimensional case) for the model weights. The model parameters are optimized via the stochastic gradient descent based Adam algorithm with learning rate 0.01.

sections.heading: 5.2Two-Dimensional ResNets
sections.text:
In this subsection, we consider ResNets to solve classification tasks for two-dimensional toy datasets. This illustrates how the universal approximation constraints of non-augmented ResNets manifest in SGD-trained networks. Specifically, the results demonstrate the “tunnel effect” in various regimes analyzed in the previous sections.

These two-dimensional experiments correspond to the approximation of the prototypical functions f : ℝ 2 → ℝ f:\mathbb{R}^{2}\to\mathbb{R} that exhibit non-degenerate critical points: Ψ circ ​ ( x 1 , x 2 ) = x 1 2 + x 2 2 − 0.5 and Ψ xor ​ ( x 1 , x 2 ) = x 2 2 − x 1 2 − 0.5 . \Psi_{\text{circ}}(x_{1},x_{2})=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}-0.5\quad\text{and}\quad\Psi_{\text{xor}}(x_{1},x_{2})=x_{2}^{2}-x_{1}^{2}-0.5. (5.20) Both have a single critical point at x 1 = x 2 = 0 x_{1}=x_{2}=0 , the function Ψ circ \Psi_{\text{circ}} a minimum, and Ψ xor \Psi_{\text{xor}} a saddle point. These two functions correspond to the prototypical toy datasets of Circle and XOR datasets via their level sets as shown in Figure 15 . For any model to successfully classify the dataset for an arbitrary number of points (Definition 2.16 ), it must effectively approximate these functions and hence embed the corresponding critical points.

sections.heading: 6Conclusion
sections.text:
In this work, we investigated the universal approximation limitations of non-augmented (or narrow) ResNets, focusing on their ability to express critical points. By parameterizing the layer update rule with a skip parameter and a residual parameter, we introduced the channel ratio α \alpha . This framing established a unified mathematical perspective on narrow ResNet architectures, and allowed us to formally bridge the gap between continuous neural ordinary differential equations and standard feed-forward neural networks. The inability to express critical points in the input-output map fundamentally restricts a network’s global approximation capabilities. We proved that the lacking expressivity forces the network to shift required critical points outside the observation domain, which leads to a “tunnel effect”. In classification tasks with nested data, this means the decision boundary always has to intersect with the domain boundary which leads to undesired misclassifications.

We systematically categorized the embedding capabilities of narrow ResNets across three distinct regimes of the channel ratio α \alpha : • The neural ODE regime ( 0 < α ≪ 1 0<\alpha\ll 1 ): When the residual channel is small relative to the skip connection, ResNets act similar to continuous neural ODEs. We provided explicit upper bounds on α \alpha below which the network strictly inherits the topological limitations of non-augmented neural ODEs, and hence is unable to embed critical points. • The MLP regime ( α ≫ 1 \alpha\gg 1 ): When the residual channel dominates, the architecture reduces to a perturbed standard feed-forward neural network. We established lower bounds on α \alpha above which the ResNet is equally incapable of expressing critical points, mirroring the constraints of standard narrow feed-forward networks. • The intermediate regime ( α ≈ 1 \alpha\approx 1 ): In this balanced regime, typical of standard ResNets, the embedding of critical points is generally possible. The numerical examples demonstrated that the ability to successfully embed a critical point remains sensitive to the precise channel ratio and magnitude of the initialized layer weights.

While non-augmented ResNets can theoretically resolve the topological restrictions of their MLP and neural ODE counterparts, this structural advantage is fragile. Because of implicit regularization effects of SGD, models initialized in sub-optimal parameter regions may converge to solutions that exhibit the tunnel effect despite having the theoretical capacity to avoid it.

SVK thanks the Munich Data Science Institute (MDSI) for partial support via a Linde doctoral fellowship. TW is supported by the Austrian Science Fund (FWF) 10.55776/J4681.
